%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.3-2 The Exponentially Varying Sinusoid

> **Objective:** Learn to read, interpret, and sketch a sinusoid whose amplitude shrinks (or grows) over time due to an exponential factor.

Consider the signal \(x(t) = 4e^{-2t}\cos(6t - 60°)\). Unlike a plain cosine, this signal does not keep a constant height — its peaks get smaller and smaller as time increases. This section teaches you to handle exactly this kind of signal.

You will learn to separate the signal into two jobs: the exponential part \(4e^{-2t}\) controls the changing amplitude, and the cosine part \(\cos(6t - 60°)\) controls the oscillation pattern. Together they produce a decaying oscillation.

For exams, these signals appear in waveform-sketching problems, transient-response questions, and parameter-reading tasks. Knowing how to split the two parts and sketch the envelope first will save you significant time.

$$x(t) = A e^{-at} \cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)$$
*The cosine still oscillates back and forth, but its peak size is no longer constant because the factor \(e^{-at}\) multiplies the entire cosine and causes the amplitude to change with time.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Three-step construction: the decaying exponential sets the envelope (left), the shifted cosine sets the oscillation pattern (center), and the final signal is the product — a sinusoid trapped between its upper and lower envelopes (right).*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777214560760-1340.png)

## 1. How to Read the Two Parts of the Signal

Every exponentially varying sinusoid \(x(t) = A e^{-at} \cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)\) has two distinct jobs:

- **\(A e^{-at}\) — the envelope factor.** This part controls how large the oscillations are at each moment. When \(a > 0\), the factor \(e^{-at}\) decreases over time, so the amplitude shrinks. A larger value of \(a\) means the envelope collapses faster.
- **\(\cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)\) — the oscillation factor.** This part controls the shape and timing of the oscillations. The angular frequency \(\omega_0\) sets how densely the cycles are packed, and \(\theta\) shifts the cosine left or right in time.

### KEY RELATIONSHIPS

The period of oscillation is \(T_0 = 2\pi / \omega_0\). The time constant of the envelope is \(\tau = 1/a\). These two numbers together tell you almost everything about the waveform's shape.

### EXAM TIP

Never try to sketch the full waveform all at once. Always identify the envelope, the period, and the phase shift first — then combine them into the final sketch. This three-step approach is faster and far less error-prone under exam conditions.

$$T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0}, \qquad \tau = \frac{1}{a}$$
*\(T_0\) tells you how far apart the oscillation cycles repeat in time, while \(\tau\) tells you how quickly the envelope rises or falls — after one time constant \(\tau\), the envelope has dropped to \(1/e \approx 37\%\) of its starting value.*

## 2. Worked Example: Sketch \(x(t) = 4e^{-2t}\cos(6t - 60°)\)

### STEP 1 — FIND THE ENVELOPE

Here \(a = 2\), so the time constant is \(\tau = 1/2 = 0.5\) s. The upper envelope is \(+4e^{-2t}\) and the lower envelope is \(-4e^{-2t}\). Evaluate the upper envelope at key times:

| \(t\) (s) | \(4e^{-2t}\) (approx) |
|-----------|----------------------|
| 0 | 4.00 |
| 0.5 | 1.47 |
| 1.0 | 0.54 |
| 1.5 | 0.20 |
| 2.0 | 0.07 |

Plot these points and draw a smooth decaying curve through them — this is your upper envelope. Mirror it below the axis for the lower envelope.

### STEP 2 — FIND PERIOD AND PHASE

Here \(\omega_0 = 6\), so \(T_0 = 2\pi/6 \approx 1\) s. The phase is \(-60°\), which is a delay of \((60/360) \times T_0 \approx (1/6) \times 1 \approx 0.167\) s. The cosine starts its first positive peak about \(1/6\) s after \(t = 0\).

### STEP 3 — COMBINE INTO THE FINAL SKETCH

Draw the shifted cosine so that its positive peaks just touch the upper envelope and its negative peaks just touch the lower envelope. The waveform is completely trapped between \(+4e^{-2t}\) and \(-4e^{-2t}\).

### EXAM SPEED NOTE

In an exam, sketch the two envelope curves first, mark the period and phase delay on the time axis, then draw the oscillation between the envelopes. You do not need to compute exact values beyond the few listed above.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="example_support" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 The decaying sinusoid \(x(t) = 4e^{-2t}\cos(6t - 60°)\) is bounded by its upper envelope \(+4e^{-2t}\) (dashed, above) and lower envelope \(-4e^{-2t}\) (dashed, below). Positive peaks touch the upper envelope; negative peaks touch the lower envelope.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777214743609-6283.png)

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- The exponential factor \(Ae^{-at}\) sets the amplitude envelope; larger \(a\) means faster decay.
- The cosine factor \(\cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)\) sets the period \(T_0 = 2\pi/\omega_0\) and phase shift.
- The final sketch is always trapped between the upper and lower envelopes \(\pm K e^{-at}\).

*In the next section we will build on this waveform intuition in a new signal form.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
